results.title: Verification of Embodied Agents - Guided Action Selection ... - note
results.content: While omnimodal LLMs, which integrate and process all modalities such as images, audio, video, and text, are expected to be the next generation of AI, this paper reveals their fundamental weakness. As the paper title 'Senses Wide Shut' suggests, it argues that while these models can 'perceive' (representation) diverse inputs, they possess a 'Representation-Action Gap' where they cannot convert them into appropriate 'actions.' In experiments targeting multiple omnimodal models, the authors demonstrated that even if models appear to understand inputs correctly, they show significantly lower performance in actual action selection tasks compared to humans or specialized models. This is not merely a lack of performance but may stem from structural design flaws in multimodal integration [...] Today's AI technology trends focus on the action reliability and self-expansion capabilities of Embodied Agents that interact with the physical world, as well as the fundamental structural challenges of multimodal large language models. In particular, approaches that insert evaluations by a Verifier before action execution to reduce errors, and frameworks where agents actively acquire unknown tools through hierarchical skill trees, represent significant steps toward the practical application of autonomous agents. Meanwhile, the representation-action gap in omni-modal LLMs, where 'perception does not lead to action,' has become apparent, necessitating a rethink of the design philosophy of multimodal AI itself. Attempts to describe VLM spatial understanding with 3D primitives and [...] stem from structural design flaws in multimodal integration architectures.

Once we have established the various cognitive processes that intervene in the determination of verbal and non-verbal behaviour of Multimodal embodied agents 187 an agent a major problem arises: how these processes interact with each other, how they inﬂuence one another. An agent, to appear believable and consistent, must respect the same expectation we would have from a human; moreover it should be consistent throughout the different facets of what makes an individual. [...] 5.6 Evaluation Finally, the problem of how to evaluate multimodal interaction with an embodied agent has to be faced.
5.6.1 Helen McBreen, James Anderson and Mervyn Jack, “Evaluating 3D embodied conversational agents in contrasting VRML retail applications” McBreen et al. designed an empirical setting to evaluate the impact of different types of agents (male versus female, casual versus formal dressing), in several applications (a cinema box-ofﬁce, a bank and a travel agency). The agents have been created using existing 3D software and were placed into a virtual environment. The agent’s body follows H-Anim speciﬁcation and by altering joint values it was possible to create four types of gesture for each agent, namely nodding, waving, shrugging and typing. [...] on ﬁngers, or repeating the same intonational contour in listing; posture shift to convey topic shift); turn-taking and back-channelling (raising hand or gaze for asking turn; nodding for providing back-channel). Information about the speaker’s emotions is conveyed by affective words and gestures, intonation, facial expression, gaze and posture. Pelachaud and Poggi argue that starting from this deductive semantic taxonomy is a useful way to start building “mode-speciﬁc” lexicons that embodied agents may be endowed with.

results.title: Sandboxed Coding Agents are Competitive Omni-modal Task Solvers
results.content:
`<answer>`

Supervised warm start.
For examples with demonstration trajectories, we fine-tune on assistant-generated tokens only, masking terminal outputs, tool responses, and environment observations from the loss. This stage teaches the basic interaction protocol: inspect files, run commands, create intermediate artifacts, recover from tool failures, and submit answers in the required format.

Reward design.
We design a process-aware verifiable reward that goes beyond binary answer matching, addressing three failure patterns: (1) the agent guesses without engaging the workspace, (2) it ignores the relevant modality (e.g., answering a video question from text alone), and (3) it emits malformed, escape-attempting, or otherwise broken tool calls. For each rollout τ\tau we compute [...] Figure 5 reports the empirical distribution of the six failure modes across four coding agents on OmniGAIA. Knowledge-Retrieval dominates the residual errors for three of four agents (Kimi 40%, Sonnet 43%, MiniMax 35%), while Codex-GPT5.4-Low is distinctive in that Tool/Environment Failure (21%) and Insufficient Exploration (20%) each rival its knowledge errors (27%). MiniMax further stands out for heavy Video-Perception errors (28%), indicating that different agents break down along different axes of the taxonomy. Beyond aggregate rates, Figure 4 dissects a single OmniGAIA trajectory as a DAG of annotated steps, showing how multiple modes from our taxonomy—audio-perception errors, rule violations, and scraping failures—co-occur with exploration-with-recovery behavior inside the same run, [...] access controls, and user consent; we note that our agents operate only on user-staged files within an isolated workspace and do not autonomously take real-world actions.

results.title: [PDF] Cosmos 3: Omnimodal World Models for Physical AI
results.content: is suboptimal and computationally wasteful. Can we instead design a single, unified model that natively addresses all essential capabilities for Physical AI agents? [...] 117 4 Cosmos 3: Omnimodal World Models for Physical AI 1. Introduction Physical AI agents perceive, reason, and take actions to interact with the real world. However, training such agents directly in the real world is slow, expensive, and could be dangerous. To overcome these bottlenecks, we must construct a training facility to enable safe and scalable learning in simulated worlds, where Physical AI agents acquire two fundamentally coupled capabilities: understanding and generation. Understanding allows an agent to infer latent representations, semantics, and dynamics from partial observations, and generation empowers the agent to predict and simulate plausible futures, anticipating how the world evolves and how the agent should take actions in response. Prior work has largely treated [...] training environment (Fig. 2). In the near term, Cosmos 3 synthesizes high-fidelity, diverse visual data to enhance training for Physical AI agents. We demonstrate how we can post-train Cosmos 3 into a better synthetic data generator in Sec. 4.2.3 and Sec. 4.2.4. Since agents perceive and interact with environments through diverse embodiments and tasks, Cosmos 3 supports task- and embodiment-specific specialization on top of a shared model. As a powerful mid-training model for Physical AI, Cosmos 3 establishes a better starting point by modeling general world dynamics and action priors while remaining highly amenable to downstream adaptation. In practice, the model can be post-trained on target data for distinct applications without architectural modifications, enabling data-driven

results.title: EgoMem: Lifelong Memory Agent for Full-duplex Omnimodal Models | OpenReview
results.content: detects dialog boundaries from omnimodal streams, and extracts necessary information to update the long-term memory. Unlike existing memory agents for LLMs, EgoMem relies entirely on raw audiovisual streams, making it especially suitable for lifelong, real-time, and embodied scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that EgoMem’s retrieval and memory management modules achieve over 95% accuracy on the test set. When integrated with a fine-tuned RoboEgo omnimodal chatbot, the system achieves fact-consistency scores above 87% in real-time personalized dialogs, establishing a strong baseline for future research.
